coredata.dc:title: A novel method for the detection of silver ions with carbon dots: Excellent selectivity, fast response, low detection limit and good applicability
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Jian-Cheng
Wang, Bei-Bei
Xu, Zi-Qiang
He, Xiao-Hang
Zou, Han-Feng
Yang, Qi-Qi
Jiang, Feng-Lei
Liu, Yi
coredata.dc:description: With the extensive application of silver containing materials in industry, the issue of release of silver ions has attracted increasing attention due to toxic effect of silver ions on the human health and environment. Traditionally, silver ions can be detected by atomic absorption spectroscopy, atomic emission spectrometry etc., which needed expensive and elaborate instruments and were high energy consumption. Therefore, economic and convenient methods are required for the effective determination of silver ions. Herein, two types of carbon dots (CDs-1 and CDs-2) have been prepared through a solvothermal synthesis method by using sucrose or citric acid and tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane as precursor, respectively. Then, CDs-1 were developed for the detection of silver ions through a colorimetric method. The detection mechanism was based on the reduction of silver ions and the formation of silver nanoparticles, which possess unique surface plasma resonance properties. CDs-1 could both act as reductants and stabilizers during the reaction for determination of silver ions. This CDs-1 based detection system could quantitatively determine silver ions in 3 min with a limit of detection (LOD) of 26 nmol/L (2.81 ppb). Interestingly, this strategy could also realize the detection of silver ions by CDs-2. Furthermore, the sensing assay is applicable to detecting silver ions in lake water and silver sulfadiazine cream samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Colorimetric sensors
Silver ions detection
Formation of silver nanoparticles
Surface plasma resonance peak

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Kinetics of reduction of silver ions
Determination of silver ions in real samples
Kinetics of reduction of silver ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science and Technology Major Project of Guangxi

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge Ke Huang for his help of measuring the AFM images of the CDs and the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21473125 ; 21573168 ; 21603067 ), Guangxi Science and Technology Project (GuiKeAD17195081) and National Infectious Diseases Project ( 2018ZX10301405 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images and the hydrodynamic diameters of CDs-1 (a), (c) and CDs-2 (b), (d). The inserts are the corresponding size distributions.
FTIR spectra of the CDs-1 (a) and CDs-2 (b).
XPS spectra and high resolution C1 s spectra of CDs-1 (a), (c) and CDs-2 (b), (d), respectively.
The UV–vis spectra of the mixture after the reaction between silver ions and CDs-1 was completed under different pH values.

TEM photographs of the silver nanoparticles obtained under different pH values by using CDs-1 as reductants and stabilizers. a, c and e: The pH values were 11, 12 and 13, respectively. The inserts are the corresponding size distributions. b, d and f: The corresponding selected area electron diffractions (pH = 11 and 12) and the high resolution TEM image (pH = 13).

The kinetics of reduction of silver ions with CDs-1 under different pH values. a, Time-resolved absorbance of silver nanoparticles at the SPR peak. b, Logarithm of the intensity at the SPR peak along with the time and the curves were well fitted by linear relationship and exponential decay model, respectively.

(a) The absorption spectra of CDs-1 incubated with different metal ions under the pH value of 13. The concentration of the metal ions was 20 μmol/L. (b) The selectivity of detection of silver ions by CDs-1. (c) The absorption spectra of CDs-1 incubated with different concentrations of silver ions under the pH value of 13. (d) The linear relationship between the absorbance of silver nanoparticles at the SPR peak and the concentration of silver ions. The absorbance intensity has subtracted the control already.
The selectivity for the detection of silver ions by CDs-1 in the presence of other metal ions. The concentrations of the metal ions were 20 μmol/L.
Detection mechanism and sensitivity of various nanosensors for detection of silver ions.

row.entry: hydroquinone reduced Ag+ to form Au@AgNPs core-shell structure
anti aggregation of AuNPs induced by Tris
Ag+ on the Au surface lead to AuNPs aggregation
Ag+ induced enhancement of fluorescence of CDs
SPR signal of UV–vis spectra of formed silver nanoparticles reduced and stabilized by CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of silver ions in commercial silver sulfadiazine cream by CDs-1.

head.ce:title: A novel method for the detection of silver ions with carbon dots: Excellent selectivity, fast response, low detection limit and good applicability

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Virology & Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education)
College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the extensive application of silver containing materials in industry, the issue of release of silver ions has attracted increasing attention due to toxic effect of silver ions on the human health and environment. Traditionally, silver ions can be detected by atomic absorption spectroscopy, atomic emission spectrometry etc., which needed expensive and elaborate instruments and were high energy consumption. Therefore, economic and convenient methods are required for the effective determination of silver ions. Herein, two types of carbon dots (CDs-1 and CDs-2) have been prepared through a solvothermal synthesis method by using sucrose or citric acid and tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane as precursor, respectively. Then, CDs-1 were developed for the detection of silver ions through a colorimetric method. The detection mechanism was based on the reduction of silver ions and the formation of silver nanoparticles, which possess unique surface plasma resonance properties. CDs-1 could both act as reductants and stabilizers during the reaction for determination of silver ions. This CDs-1 based detection system could quantitatively determine silver ions in 3 min with a limit of detection (LOD) of 26 nmol/L (2.81 ppb). Interestingly, this strategy could also realize the detection of silver ions by CDs-2. Furthermore, the sensing assay is applicable to detecting silver ions in lake water and silver sulfadiazine cream samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Silver containing materials are important for many industrial fields and products, and they have been widely used as catalysts [1], electrodes [2], antimicrobial agents [3]. However, the release of silver ions during the frequent use of silver containing materials is harmful to the human health and environment. Therefore, effective strategies for the detection of silver ions are urgently needed for the safety of drinking water, food, ecology etc. Usually, silver ions are detected by the techniques including atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [4], inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) [5,6], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [5], X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy (XRF) [7] and potentiometric methods [8]. However, complicated processes, expensive instruments and time-consuming operations are required. To overcome these problems, extensive efforts have been devoted to exploring simple and efficient methods, such as colorimetry and fluorimetry. In the past decades, various reaction-based organic molecular probes have been reported for the detection of silver ions [9]. However, it is not easy to synthesize most of these organic probes. Therefore, some easily fabricated nanomaterials have been developed to detect silver ions and it has become another hot topic.

Carbon dots (CDs), a new kind of carbon nanomaterial, which can be simply fabricated by acidic oxidation [10], hydrothermal treatment [11], electrochemistry [12], microwave heating [13], arc discharge [14], laser ablation [15] etc., have been extensively used as chemosensors for the detection of metal ions such as Hg(I) [16–18], Cu(II) [19,20], Fe(III) [21–23], Pb(II) [24], Ag(I) [25,26] and Cr(VI) [27,28], due to the “turn-on”, “turn-off”, “ratiometric response” of their fluorescence or “fluorescence resonance energy transfer” properties [29]. However, most of their intrinsic mechanisms have not been fully understood. Thus, additional novel strategies using CDs as sensors for detection of metal ions with clear mechanism and good selectivity are urgently needed.

Recently, Zheng et al. reported that sodium borohydride reduced CDs could be used as a reductant and stabilizer to synthesize gold nanoparticles at a temperature of 60 °C [30]. We also fabricated four types of CDs by a solvothermal method and the prepared CDs were used to synthesize silver nanoparticles without the addition of other reductants and stabilizer at 100 °C [31]. Later, the sulfur doped CDs were used to prepare silver nanoclusters at 80 °C in ethanol solution [32]. Wang et al. also obtained silver nanoparticles at a temperature of 50 °C using CDs derived from chitosan [33]. These successful work inspired us to enquiry whether the detection of silver ions could be realized based on the reduction of silver ions at room temperature by CDs.

It is widely accepted that silver nanoparticles possess a characteristic surface plasma resonance (SPR) peak in the UV–vis spectra. This unique property may render an excellent selectivity for the detection of silver ions based on the formation of silver nanoparticles. In addition, the absorbance of the SPR peak is linear to the concentration of silver nanoparticles in a certain range according to the lambert-beer law. Thus, it may be rational to simply detect the silver ions based on the reduction of silver ions. Herein, a label-free colorimetric nanosensor was designed to detect silver ions by CDs-1, which were derived from sucrose by a solvothermal method. The detection mechanism was based on the formation of silver nanoparticles, which has been verified by the analysis of UV–vis spectroscopy and TEM. During the formation process, CDs-1 were acted as reductants and stabilizer. In addition, the strategy could also realize the detection of silver ions by CDs-2, which were prepared through carbonization of citric acid monohydrate (CA·H2O) and tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris). Further, this sensing system was applied to well analyze the amount of silver ions in real samples.

Sucrose, CA·H2O, Tris, oleic acid and ether, sodium hydroxide etc. were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (China). Silver nitrate was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Commercial silver sulfadiazine cream was purchased from large pharmacy of Ali health from Tmall. Ultrapure water with 18.2 MΩ cm (Millipore) was used in all detection and synthesis.

CDs-1 and CDs-2 were synthesized by a solvothermal method, which has been reported by previous studies [34,35]. Briefly, 5 g of sucrose and 10 mL of oleic acid were heated at a temperature of 215 °C for 5 min under vigorously stirring and impure CDs-1 were obtained. As for CDs-2, 4 g of CA·H2O, 3 g of Tris and 8 mL of oleic acid were heated in a three neck flask equipped with a water separator under vigorous stirring at 170 °C. Much water generated and left the flask during the melting of the mixture and the temperature of the reaction system gradually reached at 215 °C, then kept this temperature for 6 min. After cooling down, the supernatant liquid was discarded and the precipitate was dispersed with water followed by extracting with ether for several times to remove the remained oleic acid. Finally, the products were freeze-dried and obtained after dialyzed in deionized water using a 1 kDa membrane for 24, 12 h respectively.

The absorption spectra of the CDs and silver nanoparticles were tested on a MAPADA double-beam spectrophotometer (MAPADA, China) at room temperature. The surface functional groups of the CDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy by a FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out with a KRATOS XSAM800 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer using Mg as the exciting source. The morphology of the CDs and silver nanoparticles was observed by a JEOL 2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of the CDs were obtained on an Ntegra Spectra AFM microscope (NT-MDTN, Russia) with tapping mode. The hydrodynamic sizes of the CDs were measured by Nano ZS90 (Malvern Instruments, Malvern, UK).

The concentration of silver ions and CDs-1 were kept at 100 μmol/L and 125 μg/mL, respectively. Sodium hydroxide was used to adjust the pH values of the reaction system. In detail, the silver ions solution was added to previously mixed CDs-1 and sodium hydroxide aqueous. Then, the mixture was stirred until the reaction was finished. Finally, the absorption spectra of the mixture were detected by a MAPDA double-beam spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetics of reduction of silver ions
ce:section.ce:para: The kinetics of the reduction of silver ions was conducted in a quartz cuvette stirred with a microliter syringe at set intervals and different concentrations of hydroxide solution (0.1, 0.01, 0.001 mol/L) were used. For instance, CDs-1 (5 μL, 50 mg/mL) was mixed with a sodium hydroxide solution (1.98 mL, 0.1 mol/L) followed by addition of a silver ions solution (20 μL, 10 mmol/L). Then, the absorption intensity of the mixture at the SPR peak was recorded.

The concentration of CDs-1 and sodium hydroxide were kept at 125 μg/mL and 0.1 mol/L, respectively. Silver ions solution was added to the previously mixed CDs-1 and sodium hydroxide to achieve the final concentration of 0–100 μmol/L. Then, the mixture was stirred for 3 min and then detected by a MAPDA double-beam spectrophotometer. The strategy for detection of silver ions was expanded to CDs-2 with optimized conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of silver ions in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The lake water was obtained from Donghu Lake of Wuhan, Hubei Province, China. The lake water was first centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min and was then filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane. The water sample was then digested by nitric acid. Finally, deionized water with various concentrations of silver ions was added and the samples were used for the measurement of silver ions by our method and ICP-AES.

Commercial silver sulfadiazine cream was first gently heated until no smoke was observed. Then, the sample was dry ashed at 500 °C for 2 h. After that, nitric acid was added to dissolve the sample with heating. The solution was heated to a droplet and then deionized water was added. Finally, the solution was filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane and prepared for the determination of the amount of silver ions by our method. ICP-AES was also used to detect the silver ions in silver sulfadiazine cream. And the silver sulfadiazine cream was treated with wet digested method by nitric acid.

CDs-1 and CDs-2 were synthesized by a solvothermal method, which has been reported by Li et al., [34] and our previous studies [31,35], using sucrose or CA·H2O and Tris as precursors. The CDs-1 and CDs-2 were prepared through “polymerization” and “carbonization” steps. Fig. 1 a and 1b show the TEM photographs of the CDs-1 and CDs-2. It can be seen that quasi-spherical and monodispersed CDs-1 and CDs-2 with narrow size distribution were obtained. The average statistical diameters of CDs-1 and CDs-2 were 2.4 ± 0.9 and 1.7 ± 0.6 nm, respectively. The size distributions of the CDs agreed well with the Gaussian distribution and the full width at half-maximum of the fitted curves were narrow (inset of Fig. 1a and b). The sizes of CDs-1 and CDs-2 were also measured by AFM (Fig. S1) and the heights of CDs-1 and CDs-2 were in the range of 2.5–3.6 nm and 1.4–3.3 nm, respectively, which were consistent with the results of TEM. Previous work also used TEM and AFM to characterize the sizes of CDs and they found that the height values of CDs measured by AFM were nearly equal to the size values measured by TEM, which was similar to our results [36–38]. Further, the hydrodynamic diameters of CDs-1 and CDs-2 were detected by dynamic light scattering (DLS). As shown in Fig. 1c and d, the hydrodynamic sizes of CDs-1 and CDs-2 were 14.8 and 11.2 nm, respectively, which were much larger than that measured by TEM and AFM. It was mainly due to their different processes involved in the preparation of sample. In the case of the TEM and AFM method, image depicted the actual size at the dried state of sample, whereas the size measured by the DLS method was a hydrodynamic diameter (hydrated state). Therefore, CDs showed a much larger hydrodynamic volume due to solvent effect in the hydrated state [39]. In addition, large amounts of oxygen functional groups were introduced onto the surface of the CDs due to the use of sucrose and CA as precursors, resulting in the strong hydrated abilities of CDs. Thus, the difference of the size of the CDs measured by TEM, AFM and DLS could become more obvious.

The UV–vis spectra of the as synthesized CDs-1 and CDs-2 were shown in Fig. S2. CDs-1 and CDs-2 possess absorption peaks at around 223 and 234 nm, respectively, which can be attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains and lead to nearly no observed PL signal. In addition, CDs-1 show absorption peak at around 280 nm, which is due to the n-π transition of the CO. Meanwhile, CDs-2 show absorption peak at 344 nm, which comes from the trapping of excited state energy of the surface states and result in strong emission. It is notable that both CDs-1 and CDs-2 do not demonstrate absorption peak at around the SPR peak of silver nanoparticles, indicating no interference would be existed during the detection of silver ions based on the reduction reaction.

The surface functional groups of the CDs-1 and CDs-2 were identified by FTIR spectra (Fig. 2 ). The broad peak centered at around 3400 cm−1 represents the stretching vibrations of OH. The sharp absorption peak at 1660 cm−1 is the stretching vibrations of CO. The sharp peak at 1730 cm−1 is associated with COOH stretching vibrations (Fig. 2b). The peaks at 1245–1260 cm−1 and 1034–1045 cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COC, respectively. And the peaks at around 2930 cm−1 and 1407 cm−1 are the stretching vibrations and bending vibrations of CH, respectively. Another two peaks at 1540 cm−1 and 1457 cm−1 are attributed to the bending vibration of NH and CN, respectively (Fig. 2b).

The surface states of CDs-1 and CDs-2 were also investigated by XPS analysis. As shown in Fig. 3 a and b, both of the CDs-1 and CDs-2 show two apparent peaks centered at 284.8 and 532.3 eV, which can be attributed to the binding energy of C 1 s and O 1s, respectively. The peak at 399.8 eV is associated with the binding energy of N 1s, indicating CDs-2 was nitrogen doped (Fig. 3b). The high resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s for CDs-1 can be fitted into four peaks, corresponding to CC at 284.6 eV, CC at 285.6 eV, COH at 286.3 eV and CO at 287.9 eV (Fig. 3c). While the C1s spectrum of CDs-2 can be fitted into CC/CC at 284.7 eV, CN/COH at 286.2 eV, CO at 287.8 eV and COOH at 288.9 eV (Fig. 3d). The results agree well with that of the FTIR of CDs-1 and CDs-2. The results of FTIR and XPS both indicate that CDs-1 and CDs-2 are functionalized with oxygen groups. Our pervious study has shown that the oxygen related functional group could act as reductant and stabilizer in the process of synthesis of silver nanoparticles [32]. Therefore, it is rational to detect silver ions by oxygen abundant CDs through generating silver nanoparticles.

As is well known, the temperature and the pH values could greatly affect the rate of reduction of silver ions. Increasing the temperature and the pH values could accelerate the reduction of silver ions [40]. In order to easily determine the amounts of silver ions, the room temperature was chosen in this study. The effects of the pH values on the reduction of silver ions were firstly studied. None doped CDs-1 was chosen to conduct the experiment. Fig. 4 shows the absorption spectra of the products after the reaction was terminated. It can be seen that the absorption spectra were not changed when the pH values was lower than 11, indicating no silver nanoparticles were formed under these conditions. In contrast, intense absorption peaks at around 400 nm were observed when the pH values were set as 11, 12 and 13. This absorption peak is the characteristic SPR peak of spherical silver nanoparticles. Thus, we could conclude that only if the pH value is higher than 11, will the reduction of silver ions proceed successfully at room temperature. In addition, the absorption peak displays a slightly blue shift with the pH value increase from 11 to 13, which may be due to the decrease of the sizes of silver nanoparticles.

To verify the speculation, TEM characterization were conducted. Fig. 5 a,c and e show the TEM images of silver nanoparticles synthesized under the pH values of 11, 12 and 13, respectively. It can be seen that well dispersed silver nanoparticles were formed. The statistical sizes of the silver nanoparticles synthesized under the pH value of 11, 12 and 13 were 7.4 ± 1.7 nm, 6.5 ± 1.5 nm and 3.5 ± 0.4 nm, respectively (Inset of Fig. 5a,c and e). The sizes of the silver nanoparticles decreased obviously as the pH value increase from 11 to 13, which agreed well with the results of the absorption spectra. Meanwhile, the silver nanoparticles were spherical and were with narrow size distribution. In addition, the size distributions of these monodispersed silver nanoparticles agreed well with Gaussian fittings and the full widths at half-maximum were narrow. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns (Fig. 5b and d) of the nanoparticles showed four Debye-Scherrer rings corresponding to (111), (200), (220) and (311) planes of a face centered cubic (fcc) crystalline lattice of silver, which further indicated the formation of silver nanoparticles. The Debye-Scherrer rings were not perfect due to the sizes of the silver nanoparticles were too small. Interestingly, the (110) plane was also observed, which typically found in silver wires or rod [41]. However, all silver nanoparticles were almost spherical particles. Therefore, the appearance of (110) plane might be due to the aggregation growth of the silver nanoparticles [42]. Fig. 4f shows the HRTEM of the silver nanoparticles synthesized under the pH value of 13. The interlayer spacings were about 0.24 nm and 0.20 nm, respectively, which were in good agreement with the d spacings of the crystal plane of (111) and (200) for fcc Ag.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetics of reduction of silver ions
ce:section.ce:para: As the pH values could efficiently affect the rate of reduction of silver ions. To rapidly detect the silver ions, the kinetics of reduction of silver ions under different pH values were investigated. Fig. 6 shows that silver ions (100 μmol/L) could be completely reduced by CDs-1 in 535, 205 and 180 s at the pH value of 11, 12 and 13, respectively. The logarithm of the intensity at the absorption peak was measured and it demonstrated a linear relationship between ln A and t until the reaction was completed when the pH values were 12 and 13, meaning that the reaction rates under pH value of 12 and 13 were exponential. When the pH value was 13, the reaction was completed in about 180 s (3 min), further indicating CDs-1 might rapidly detect silver ions through the generation of silver nanoparticles. When the pH value was changed to 11, the relationship between ln A and t was an exponential decay model, demonstrating the reaction rate slowed down along with the time. In addition, the reduction rates under pH values of 13 and 12 were much faster than that of reduction process under pH = 11. What is more, the reduction rate under pH value of 13 was a little faster than the rate under pH = 12. So, pH value of 13 was chosen in the following procedures of detection of silver ions.

Before the qualitatively analysis of the amount of silver ions, the selectivity of the detection of the silver ions was investigated. Cayuela et al. used passivate-CDs hydrogels to detect silver ions by quenching the fluorescence of the CDs, while the quenching effect of Hg(II) was nearly identical to that of the Ag(I) [43]. Tabaraki et al. also used N-doped CDs to determine the amounts of silver ions by fluorescence quenching method. However, the fluorescence of the CDs was also quenched by other metal ions such as Fe(III), Fe(II) and Hg(II) [44]. Therefore, selectivity is still a great challenge in the detection of metal ions by bare fluorescent CDs through their fluorescence properties. Fig. 7 a shows the absorption spectra of CDs-1 in the presence of various of metal ions under the pH value of 13. The SPR peak appeared only if the silver ions were added, which demonstrated an excellent selectivity for detection of silver ions with our method. As shown in Fig. 7b, it was obvious that the mixtures did not show obvious absorption at around the SPR peak with the addition of the other metal ions, indicating that they did not interfere with detection of silver ions. Fig. 8 shows the detection of silver ions in the presence of different interfering metal ions. All these metal ions did not show obvious interference in the detection of silver ions, demonstrating good selectivity during the detection of silver ions in complicated system. Fig. 7c shows that the absorbance intensity of silver nanoparticles increases with the addition of silver ions. A new peak at around 400 nm appeared in all the samples, which was due to the formation of spherical silver nanoparticles. The absorbance intensity of the silver nanoparticles at SPR peak was linear to the added concentration of silver ions (Fig. 7d). And the R 2 could reach at 0.995, demonstrating a good accuracy for detection of silver ions. Meanwhile, the detection process only needed 3 min and the limit of detection (LOD) was 26 nmol/L (3σ/S, ∼2.81 ppb), superior to the most other nanosensors reported previously (Table 1 ), indicating this method possesses excellent selective detection for silver ions. It is notable that the LOD is considerably lower than the maximum allowable level of silver ions (50 ppb) in drinking water as regulated by the WHO [45] and the limit of the 0.46 μmol/L set by the U.S. EPA [46], demonstrating this reaction-based method may have great potential in practical use. Furthermore, this method only requires an UV–vis spectrophotometer, which is greatly economical and more convenient than ICP-MS, ICP-AES, AAS, fluorescence spectrophotometer etc. Therefore, it is more applicable in practical detection.

Since the detection of silver ions is based on the reduction of silver ions, we wonder whether this method could be expanded to other types of CDs. The CDs-2 also show excellent selectivity for the detection of silver ions based on this reaction based strategy (Fig. S3). However, for the CDs-2, the LOD of silver ions was 17 nmol/L (1.84 ppb) and it needed 30 min to complete the detection process, which was 10 times longer than the time used by CDs-1. This may be caused by the different reducibility of the two types of CDs, which is dominated by the surface functional groups. According to the results of FTIR and devolution of C 1s XPS spectra of the two types of CDs, CDs-2 contain some COOH and CDs-1 mainly composed of OH, which may be the origin for the different behavior of the reaction-based detection of silver ions. Considering this, another type of CDs containing large amounts of COOH was attempted to determine silver ions under the same condition. And no silver nanoparticles were formed (results not shown), which further verified our speculation. Thus, OH should be a dominated factor for the reaction-based detection strategy and CDs used for the detection of silver ions could be further optimized by modifying their surface states in future studies.

The applicability of this sensing system for detection of silver ions in real samples was further evaluated. Lake water and commercial silver sulfadiazine cream were used and the results were listed in Tables S1 and Table 2 . After spiked with different amounts of silver ions into lake water, the determined amounts were consistent with the results obtained by the ICP-AES method and the recoveries were located in the range of 95–105%, indicating the developed colorimetric nanosensor possesses potential applicability for practical water analysis. In addition, commercial silver sulfadiazine cream, a complicated real sample, was also used to verify the validity of our sensing system. According to the user guide, the content of silver sulfadiazine cream is 0.1 g/g, conversed to the silver ion, is 3.024 × 10−3 g/g. By our sensing system, the determined value is 3.108 ± 0.0587 × 10−3 g/g, which agrees well with the result obtained by ICP-AES, indicating this reaction-based sensing system possesses excellent validity for real sample analysis.

In summary, CDs-1 prepared through a solvothermal method via sucrose as precursor has been developed as a colorimeteric sensor for detection of silver ions at room temperature. The detection mechanism is based on the formation of silver nanoparticles, which possesses unique SPR properties. During the reaction, CDs-1 can directly reduce silver ions to form silver nanoparticles without adding other reducers or stabilizers. The CDs-1 based sensing system is economic and rapid since it only needs an UV–vis spectrophotometer and it may allow one to qualitatively detect silver ions within 3 min with a LOD of 26 nmol/L (2.81 ppb). In addition, this strategy can be efficiently applied in the determination of silver ions in lake water and cream samples and could be also applicable to CDs-2 preparing from CA and Tris. Moreover, this finding may open a gate to expand detection fields of CDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge Ke Huang for his help of measuring the AFM images of the CDs and the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No.21473125; 21573168; 21603067), Guangxi Science and Technology Project (GuiKeAD17195081) and National Infectious Diseases Project (2018ZX10301405).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.04.036.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visible-light-enhanced catalytic oxidation reactions on plasmonic silver nanostructures
Nat. Chem.
A transparent electrode based on a metal nanotrough network
Nat. Nanotechnol.
Silver as antibacterial agent: ion, nanoparticle, and metal
Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.
Determination of metal content in honey by atomic absorption and emission spectrometries
Trends Anal. Chem.
Analytical techniques for the determination of precious metals in geological and related materials
Analyst
Determination of essential and potentially toxic trace elements in honey by inductively coupled plasma-based techniques

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jian-Cheng Jin received his BS degree from Wuhan University in 2013. Since then, he became a doctoral student in professor Yi Liu’s laboratory at College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University. He is interested in synthesis of carbon dots and their applications.

Bei-Bei Wang received her BS degree from Zhengzhou University in 2015. She then became a doctoral student in professor Yi Liu’s laboratory at College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University. Her research is related to the preparation of carbon dots and their biological application.

Zi-Qiang Xu obtained his PhD in 2015 from College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University. Since 2015, he became a lecturer of School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University. His research is related to the preparation of carbon dots and their applications, especially in the biomedical fields.
Xiao-HangHe is a junior student in Wuhan University of Technology. He is interested in the preparation of carbon dots.

Han-Feng Zou obtained her BS degree in 2017 from Wuhan University. She began carbon dots study in 2015 in Prof. Yi Liu’s Lab until graduation. She now focuses on fabrication of quantum dots and their application in display in Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology.

Qi-Qi Yang obtained her BS degree from Weifang University in 2013. She then became a doctoral student in professor Yi Liu’s laboratory at College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University. Her research is related to the synthesis of carbon dots and their biological effect.

Feng-Lei Jiang obtained his BS (2003) and PhD (2008) degree in chemistry under the supervision of Prof. Yi Liu from Wuhan University. In this period, he was also an exchange student under the supervision of Prof. Rick W. K. Wong at Hong Kong Baptist University. He worked with Prof. Pierre D. Harvey as a postdoctoral fellow at Université de Sherbrooke in Canada from October 2008 to November 2009. Then he came back to Wuhan University to work as a Lecturer. He was promoted Associate Professor in November 2011. Currently, his research focuses on fluorescent molecular and nano probes for biomedical applications.

Yi Liu received his BS and PhD degree from College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences at Wuhan University. He is currently a professor at the College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences in Wuhan University and his research focuses on the synthesis, biological effect and application of quantum dots.
